segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: Listen to a talk on an astronomy podcast.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: When I was growing up, Pluto was known as the ninth planet in our solar system.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: Alas, Pluto was demoted to dwarf planet status in 2006.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: And not only because it's so small, maybe half the width of the United States.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: It's mainly because in 2006, it was decided that planets had to meet three criteria.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: They had to orbit the sun and be round in shape, check and check.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: But they also had to be the main object in their orbit, which, well, Pluto is in the Kuiper Belt region, in the far outer reaches of the solar system, with many other bodies that orbit the sun.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: So Pluto lost its planet status.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: As a result, many scientists dismissed Pluto as just a ball of ice anyway.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: However, about ten years later, the New Horizons spacecraft sent back new images and data showing that Pluto is far more complex than previously thought.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: Pluto has a deep basin with three types of ice, and New Horizons images suggest there's convection in the basin of ice.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: With convection, warmer ice rises to the surface, then cools and sinks at the edge of the basin, so the surface is continuously rejuvenating, renewing itself.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: Moreover, under the basin of ice may be something astonishing.

segments.speaker: Host
segments.text: This means many neighboring bodies in the Kuiper Belt could also have oceans and potentially be habitable.
